speaker: interviewer
text: I'm the interviewer, Donna Canter. The data is March 16, 1995, and this is tape four. Let's-- you were saying that you decided you should sing,

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, like I said, you know-- I have to qualify that, that we did arrive to Birkenau. We considered it Auschwitz everywhere, but this was Birkenau. And it was B lager, and it was Block 16. And I was-- there were a lot of people in there, and when we arrived, and the people from our camp asked me to sing, I felt obliged. Because I figure, these people that were next to me, there was on one side-- and I think I probably went over more than one person, must have continued on. And if they asked me to sing, I better oblige them and sing to make up for what I did to them every night, practically. Because all they were giving us is water and-- and just, they called it soup. And that night, they give us, like, coffee, they called it, which was like mud. And so, I felt obliged to sing. So I got up, and I sang That's the song I learned from that old man in Ostrowiec. And I sang it, and after it was through, I heard applause, and then suddenly stop, and it and got very quiet, and I turned around, and there was the head of my camp, my block. My block eldester, they used to call it, somebody that was in charge of your block. She was a Jewish young woman who came from Czechoslovakia, and had a big ego on her, because she's been there for a long time, and considered us as privileged, and she showed it to us, that we were privileged, at every appel. She would make sure that somebody got it good. She wasn't very kind, she wasn't-- she-- she wanted to show to the Germans that she has authority.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when I got up to sing, and I finished, and they started applauding, but the minute they saw her, everybody stopped. I looked around, and I said, uh-oh, that's it. Well, in the meantime, she started applauding, so everybody joined in, and she asked me to come into her room afterwards.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I went to her room, and I became privileged, very privileged. I got extra food, and she asked me to-- to sing for her and her assistant. And it seems in the block, there was a-- a block eldester, and an assistant block eldester. And they had a room all their own at the-- at the beginning of the block, which was right where that chimney went up, just before. It had-- it had a room, and they had their own bunks. And she asked me if I could come into her room, and I could sing to them, and if I know any songs-- any other songs. And after all, I had two sisters, and they were already 18 and 16 at the time when they were taken away, so I heard a lot of love songs, even though we were a religious family at home. But they knew a lot of Polish love songs, and I-- like, I was very lucky. I really absorbed everything, and I learned their songs. So I said, sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: So after that, I used to go into their room, and I would sing to them, and I would get little crumbs, little food, extra food. And I would bring it back for my mother. And I was very privileged.

speaker: interviewee
text: And she asked me if it would be OK for her to take me to see the lager eldester, which is the head of the camp, the Jewish head of the camp. And now, there was another privileged person, and I said, sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: So just before she took me to see-- I'm talking about time elapsing. It didn't happen like, today, and later and-- it happened during time. I'm talking about time. Because when we got there, none of us did anything. It was-- we got up in the morning, we went to appel. When, at the crack of dawn, we would have appel.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we would stand in appel, and as I was saying, I learned how-- where to stand, even though we had our own designated spot, and we had to stand in our own designated spot. I had found my designated spot to be in the middle, and not at the end, and not in the back, and not at the front. And at every appel, somebody would get a beating. Somebody didn't stand right. Somebody didn't do whatever it was that had to be done. And we would stand there until a German-- a German would come by, and it used to be women. In our block, at least, there was a woman that would come by, and she was dressed just like any other officer, and with those clanking of the boots, and she would go with her poke, and count us. The same way as our block eldester would count, previously. She would make sure everybody's there. And-- and-- and the German, she would tell the German, everybody's here. But most of the time, she would count us again, the German officer. And she would count us, and walk through the group, look at us, and then at the will of our block eldester, we would either stand there or be dismissed. Sometimes you would stand there for extended length of time for no apparent reason that I knew of. And later on, we would get, and we would go and get something. I don't remember if the bread came at the end of the day, beginning of the day, or in the middle of the day, whatever. We would get something in our stomachs then. And then we would go back into our block, and we would do nothing. We would do nothing. There was nothing for us to do in Auschwitz. We were just waiting around. There was absolutely nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when she asked me-- when-- when she asked me to come and see the head of the camp, we had since gone to another shower. [INAUDIBLE] shower. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Oh, I forgot to say. I'm sorry.

speaker: interviewee
text: I forgot to say, I should really reiterate what our day was like. So we would go-- we would go through the appel, and then the day was nothing. And then we would have an appel in the evening again, I guess at sundown, or whatever it was, I don't remember. We would have another appel, and the same thing would-- we would go through, exactly the same. We would be counted. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: During those appels, quite a few people lost their lives, and it was due to our block eldester. Like one time, she did something, I don't remember what it was. She made-- made her kneel down, and hold a chair in her hand, and naturally, how long can you hold a chair? So she beat her to death, they took her away. And there was quite a few, quite a few. I mean, it was always something that wasn't pleasing.

speaker: interviewee
text: And she just wanted-- I felt she wanted to show for the Germans that she was efficient and she was doing her job. I don't know what it was. Or her anger, the fact that she's been there all this time, or whatever it was. And she was a young woman. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Looking back, maybe in her late 20s, early 30s, middle 30s, I don't remember. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But this went on day in and day out. And we would periodically go to the bathroom as a group, and the bathroom was one barrack made out of holes, you know, whatever. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] She asked me to go to the head of the camp. But just before that, we went through another scrubbing. I mean, going to the showers. And I must have stood a little longer at the shower, because when I got through, all I got is a shirt. All they were able to give me was a shirt. And my mother got a slip, because she was waiting for me. So she got a slip, like a cotton slip or something, and I got a shirt. No underwear, no nothing, just a shirt for my mother-- um, for me, and a slip for my mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when the block-- when the-- when I went to hear the block eldester, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] all I had is that shirt. And I was wearing it like Tarzan, around my middle, and I tied the sleeve behind me. And I was Tarzan, the woman of apes or something.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I went to sing. Somebody gave me something to put on top. One of the women gave me something, because they heard, and they were all-- they were also impressed that I got a chance to go and sing for the head of the camp. Well, when I got there, I sang for her all the love songs I remembered my sisters singing, and they were all very nice, and they took another girl along with me too. She was also from our hometown. She was bigger than me. Everybody was bigger than me. I mean, at least my own age, they were taller and bigger. By that time, I was already 11 years old. And I remember, when she got through with me, she told me she was going to take me and get me dressed. So I told her, I said, I would-- I need some clothes for my mother, too. And I wouldn't want to be dressed-- I mean, I want her to have some clothes too. Well, needless to say, I don't know if a day passed by, or two, or three, or whatever time. She took me to the storeroom, where there were-- I can't tell you. It was like going into a Paris shop. You-- you can dress as you will. And-- and they dressed me up like I walked out of a showroom, with a coat and a dress and shoes and stockings and god knows what. And I brought back for my mother shoes and clothes and everything. Well unfortunately, I don't know how long this lasted, but it was only like days before they took us out of that B lager. They took the children and separated us completely and took us away. And that's the time-- every time you were separated, you went through a delousing and a shower. Every time. So here were my clothes, and I remember my tears running down my face because I had to give up my clothes. I finally got some clothes on my back. And they took us, they dressed us. It was better than average kind of clothing that they gave us. And they took us into a block, and I don't know to this day in which camp or where it was. But it was an interim block. It was a small, little block, and we had white linens, and we had bedding, and we had nurses, and we had [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] everything. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And they-- they made us go out in a yard, which was part of the-- part of the-- part of our block, a part of our barrack, and they taught us how to sing and to dance, and we found out later that a group of-- of the Red Cross was coming through the camp, and we were supposed to be the show-off kind of thing. I remember the nurses wearing white. I remember they examined us. I remember people were teaching us how to sing, and I remember we were learning-- I remember, we learned a Polish song, we learned a-- a Czech dance outside, and-- and a German. We learned how to dance and to sing, and we had to do it. And we got food served in-- in different kind of containers. I mean, it was incredible. It's like we-- we died and went to heaven somewhere. But doing that particular time, after we left the B lager, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] the-- the ovens were going day and night. I mean, throughout my stay, it was going day and night, but after-- especially after we left, it never stopped. I mean, the-- the skies were so red, and burning red day and night. It just didn't make a difference whether it was night or day. The sky was-- the skies were afire. It was unbelievable. Unbelievable. And I remember always looking up, and I had my own little dreams. I used to like watch and see the clouds, and-- and it was like bodies were floating by-- I mean, in my head. I remember those-- those-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] those clouds of red. And the-- and the stench was so thick, it stuck to your body. You could never get rid of it. You would-- after a while, you didn't even know the difference, because this is the way it smelled, everything. But you could almost touch it, because it was like-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] like if somebody poured glue over you or something. It was always-- you were always thick. And-- and that's-- that's when the ovens were going day and night, and night to day, during that time [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] when we were taken away. And we weren't very long in that little camp, because they took us-- after that, they took us to the Zigeunerlager, and to-- which was the Camp E. And, just the children's block.

speaker: interviewer
text: The gypsy camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Zigeuner is the gypsy camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right, the gypsy camp. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] a lot of twins were brought in that gypsy camp, a lot of twins. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I remember, they were, like, coming in the morning, and taken away, and never coming back. Very few ever came back. They were just like taken away in the morning, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] very few came back. And I remember there were a lot of kids that came and never came back. And our-- our barrack looked different than the others. I don't remember exactly what it looked like, but it didn't look the same as the other barracks. And it was block 17, I mean seven. Number seven. I had something about numbers, that I remember. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] when we got to that camp, like I say, there were a lot of twins then taken away, and it was a lot of elimination taking place as we were coming in to that camp. I noticed in our block, and there were not too many-- too many blocks occupied in that camp, not too many. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] During that time when I was in there, I found my brother. My oldest brother was right across in the next camp across from us, in D lager. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And-- one-- one day, he got a hold of somebody from our hometown, and-- and they told me-- to get me, and I went near-- See, the end of the-- the end of the barrack was also a door to go out, and you faced the-- the electric fences on that side. The front side is where-- where the walkway, where the barracks sort of head to head each other. This was the back side of the barrack. On the backside of the barracks were the electric fences on either side. So there were even and odd barracks. And we were on the odd side, and we were right next to the D camp, which was the men's camp, and that's where my brother was. And he got a hold of somebody that told me, and I found my brother. And every once in a while, we used to able to see each other through that back. And one time he told me, I didn't know, but he told me that my mother was in the hospital in that camp. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Well, I took it upon myself to go there. I wasn't supposed to leave my-- my barrack, but I did go, and I found my mother. She was sick in that hospital. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And my brother [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [SIGHS] He brought some food. Because he used to work at Buna, which was the working Auschwitz camp also. And used to work at the-- air-- I think it was airlines, or whatever they had there, and they were doing something for the Germans. And he passed me along the electric-- we were so dumb, so crazy. He-- we risked our lives. The first time he passed me along a little, like, almost the size of a salt shaker. It was a little container that had a cap on it. And it smelled kind of funny inside, but there was salt inside it. And that was, to me, it was like heaven. Because I used to lick it. So I guess-- I-- I don't know what it did, but it did a lot of good. [LAUGHS] I used to lick that salt. And then when he found out that mother was sick, he gave me across one time, like a pudding, A rice pudding or some kind of pudding. And I wanted to eat it so badly. Here was the sentry standing. Here is the electric wires. And if you touched it, you were a goner, and there was-- the space was little. And we-- he gave me in a [NON-ENGLISH], which is a-- like an army-- I don't know. An army container. They're made out of aluminum or whatever, or some kind of metal, which conducts electricity even worse, I think. And he passed me that along for mother. -- And I-- I took it. And we were lucky. I didn't touch any electric wires, because ordinarily, I'm pretty klutzy. And here is the sentry standing. And here I am, taking that across. I cannot believe that we did that, but we did. We did a lot of things. I know I'm making everything very light, and I'm not really going into-- into the heaviness of it, maybe because I was a child, and these are my memories. And all the bad ones, I want-- I just put behind me. All-- all the-- the dead bodies that ended up on those wires every single morning, that were cleaned up. Every single morning I-- I stayed in Auschwitz. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Or-- or those children that never returned, or our regular tortures every-- every time. I seem to have put behind me, and only remember the incidents. But I know, as I'm talking, I realize that everything sounds so light and so breezy. It's like, you're going-- you're going away, and you're telling somebody about your trip. But it wasn't. It really wasn't. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It really wasn't. I just want people to know that it wasn't a trip. That it was real hard to survive every single day [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] of my-- of my three and a half years, was a real survival effort, every single day. Every minute that I survived was an effort. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] As far as getting that-- that container to my mother, I did. Now, it was no easy deed. If I would've been caught, I wouldn't be here either. Everything we did was a risk. Every time, we either-- whether we slept, or we got up, or whether we got out, or we got in, it was just a matter of being in the right place at the right time. It didn't matter. It didn't make sense. There was no sense to anything.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it doesn't matter whether I took that over and risked my life, or whether I stayed in the block and just stayed there. It didn't make a difference. Every day was a struggle. Every day was a survival effort. And [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] this is-- this is how we survived. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember doing something very heroic one time, in that block seven. A truck had pulled over, with-- the bread truck. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I saw one of the breads falling under the truck. Not supposed to be there, first of all. I risked my life, and I crawled under the truck, and I picked up that bread. And when I got out of there, all the kids attacked me and took away my bread. I didn't even get one little piece left. I was so devastated. I was so angry that here, I risked myself, my life, and I didn't even get the chance to taste it. They took everything away from me. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] It was just-- and if we got a chance to-- to run over near the kitchen, and there were very seldom any potato peels, because the potato peels probably were thrown into the soup, and the soup was really not soup, it was like water. So if we got that, it was a major find, if you find a potato peel somewhere.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's pick up with the other events there, when we change tapes.

speaker: interviewer
text: You lose three minutes each tape.
